sentences.text: Today is October the 30th, 1996.
My name is Leonie Lachmish, and I have the honor of interviewing Mr.
Norman Tuckman today, whose previous name was Tuchmann.
The interview is taking place in Ashkelon, in Israel, and we're going to be speaking in English.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Norman Tuckman.
Norman.
N-O-R-M-A-N.
OnIn Tuckman, T-U-C-K-M-A-N.
Tuchmann.
T-U-C-H-M-A-N-N.
Hebrew name, Nissan.
It's a first na-- it's the first month.
N-I-S-S-A-N.
I did have a name, Leonard Neidhard.
It's a lengthy name.
L-E-O-N-A-R-D Neidhard, N-E-I-D-H-A-R-D.
No.
No.
What's your date of birth?
17th of November, 1922.
Munich, Germany.
No.
They both came from a small town in Poland called Teschen.
It's in Galicia.
Simply for economic reasons.
At that time, it was difficult for my father to find parnassa in Poland, and they moved.
The turn of the century.
The story goes, my father's father, they all lived in Poland.
They all lived in the same town, and I feel the small town, that consisted of approximately 200 people, either 100 or 150 were Tuchmann or Tuchmann [INAUDIBLE].
The whole family springs from there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father's father found it almost impossible to earn living for his family, and he followed his brother to America.
This is all only hearsay because we have no factual evidence.
My father joined his brother, and the very same night as he arrived, both were murdered.
My father and his family were left literally destitute and they moved to Germany and lived there.
My father's father.
My--
In America.
No.
No.
The bro-- my grandfather's brother went on ahead, and he wrote back and say, why don't you come out to America?
It is much easier for you to earn his-- your living here.
And he went out and, so we understand, the very first night he arrived there, they were both murdered.
No.
No.
That's all we know.
Yes.
My father's occupation was a master tailor.
There was a difference between a tailor and a master tailor.
A master tailor was a person who could design suits and dresses, cut the pattern, cut the material, and do the stitching.
As such, he was reasonably successful, yes.
My father's name was Nathan Naftalli, and my mother's name was Regina.
Regina.
Also Tuchmann.
Yes.
But distantly-- distantly related.
I have a-- had a sister.
Her name was Fanny.
She was born in '27. 1927.
The Jewish community of Munich split into two sections.
There were basically what you would call the German Jews, or in Israel we call them the [INAUDIBLE].
And there were the Polish Jews.
That was the community I belonged to.
We were not the second class citizens, but I would rather say the third class citizens.
We lived very much together.
We prayed together.
We had our own synagogue.
There was no such thing as being orthodox.
We all were orthodox.
There was nothing else for us to do.
We grew into this life.
The synagogue was part of our life.
We had very little contact with the German Jewish community.
All the people in our synagogue were Jews who originally came from Poland.
We all speak German.
Everybody spoke German.
Everybody spoke German.
Yiddish was spoken amongst my parents and their friends.
We did not learn Yiddish, but we acquired a certain sense for Yiddish.
The Jewish community was reasonably well organized.
We had a central office that dealt with all Jews of Germany-- German and Polish and Russian and what else.
Beyond that, there was a Jewish school, a [INAUDIBLE], all facilities.
Jewish sports club.
It was well organized.
No.
I don't.
I went to a Jewish school, yes.
Judische Volksschule Munchen.
And that means Jewish Elementary School, Munich.
I'm trying-- now trying to relate.
I think from 1928, '29 till '36, '37.
General education.
Probably more than an outside school, but by no means exclusively Jewish.
The children who were all Jewish.
The teachers were all Jewish, as far as I remember.
Reasonably good.
We lived in a building.
We were the only Jewish family, and all families around us were non-Jewish.
Not-- not before 1933.
Not at all.
No.
Our home life was a happy one.
We were a simple family.
We had no great luxuries.
It is pretty well the same as it's here.
[INAUDIBLE] Shabbat, we all went to synagogue.
Perhaps not my mother so much, but certainly my father.
We came back and we made Kiddush and we sing-- we sung [INAUDIBLE].
It was no different than it is here.
In my later years, I was a choir boy.
Shabbat morning-- in the synagogue.
Shabbat morning, everybody went to synagogue.
When I see everybody, at least the people that I knew, all went to synagogue?
The food.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't precisely remember, but the choice wasn't that we have today.
One ate [INAUDIBLE].
We always said if you had a lot of-- if you had money, you had more meat in the [INAUDIBLE], and if you had no money, you had [INAUDIBLE].
Mainly boys' books written for my generation.
Westerns and such.
I was extremely interested in communication in radio.
And as my parents couldn't afford a radio, I joined a club-- a radio club-- and eventually made one.
In Munich.
We are now talking when I was 12, 13.
My greatest wish for my bar mitzvah is to have a radio.
And eventually I did have a crystal set, which is unknown to our children today.
It was a crystal set where you had to twiddle to get a station, and you listened with earphones.
Yes.
I remember one was most concerned because I was just about to leave school to go into a gymnasium, which was a higher education.
And whilst we set our tests, we were rejected, being Jewish.
And we returned to our basic school.
One did hear, because we had a infamous concentration camp near Munich-- it was Dachau-- and anybody who didn't toe the line, Jews and non-Jews, went there.
Well, there were other cases.
Besides religious, there were political, criminal cases.
They were all dispatched to Dachau.
We are now talking '33, '34, '35.

My first experience was in Munich, when a young solicitor-- Jewish solicitor-- known to my parents, was marched through the town with a big placard around his neck-- I'm a Jew, I had committed something, something.
And he was promptly sent to Dachau, and returned very soon after in a leaded coffin.
But at that time, anybody killed in a concentration camp was returned to the Jewish community for burial in Munich.
Yes, some returned alive, yes.
Oh, yes.
No.
No.
I remember life became-- becoming very uncertain and very unsettled, and my parents made every strenuous effort to emigrate.
But nobody would have us.
So eventually we did obtain an affidavit to go to United States.
And we prepared for immigration.
That was now going on for '30-- 1937.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Suitcases were packed, everything was ready for immigration.
And our cases were dispatched to be collected in the port of Antwerp, Holland.
And there they remained.
Antwerp in Holland.
Everything was dispatched for emigration to America.
We had obtained our tickets.
We had our affidavits.
We were just waiting for our number.
We were in numbers.
The Americans would accept so many per year, and we were waiting for our number to come up.
War started before our number came up.
Our cases had been sent in advance.
Everything was in Antwerp port-- in a free port-- ready to go.
When I say ready to go, don't imagine that we had 50 large cases.
No, no.
We had perhaps three or four cases.
A sewing machine, little bit of furniture, clothing, and whatever my father needed.
Nobody wanted us.
We tried everywhere.
They tried Cuba, they tried China, they tried everywhere.
Jews were then anxious to leave, to leave the best-- wherever they could.
Nobody wanted us.
One did hear rumors and the minute you-- you-- you had some information, you went to the consulate.
People often sat outside the consulate all night waiting for the consulate to open to find information, to find somewhere to go.
Yes.
It became-- became rather distant, not in a-- an enemy-- not a-- not a-- not a hostile feeling towards us, but we had very little contact with the German community?
To give you an example, on the same landing where we lived, the family opposite us, he was a great stamp collector, and so was I.
Great stamp collector.
I had about 200 stamps, but he had 10,000.
And so I spent many a day and evening with him, he explaining his stamp collection and I learned a great lot.
After '33 or '34, this contact stopped.
I used to see him and say good morning and good afternoon, and that was the end.
And that applied to everybody.
Not much, because my father was in partnership with a non-Jew.
The business was in a non-Jewish name.
My father handled many more Jews who were emigrating who came to buy suits and have suits made.
But beyond that, little changed.
Didn't change at all.
He was an extremely honest, decent German citizen.
No.
No, no, no, no.
No, not at all.
Not at all.
I don't know-- first of all, he was an old man then, and I-- I've no contact with anybody.
Yes, Multsera.
Frants Multsera.
Yes.
Yes.
Perhaps it was already getting on for '38, but between '37 and '38, yes.
From my point of view, from-- first of all, we were not accepted in a non-Jewish school.
We returned to our school, but basically the German sch-- the Jewish school that I went to only had four classes-- four elementary classes in Germany.
Now, the Jewish community in Munich was confronted with a problem, that the children could not be accepted, nor the teachers.
And so they founded four additional classes.
That was easier said than done.
First of all, we had to find premises adjoining to the previous school.
We had makeshift classes, but a fairly good education.
The Jewish community.
Yes.
The Jewish Gemeinde Community.
Yes.
Very little.
There was no such thing as a big bar mitzvah party.
We had-- but we-- my bar mitzvah took place traditionally.
The party was held at home.
Friendly, but tense.
I remember the tension.
We didn't think how things were going to turn out.
We only thought how soon could we leave.
No.
The next incident happened in Germany.
There was a great shock to us all when all Polish Jews were collected to be deported.
Germany felt that Polish Jews had no place in Germany, no matter how long they lived there.
And from one day to the next, all Polish Jews were collected to be deported.
Fortunately-- in 19-- perhaps '38.
Fortunately my family and the Polish console were great friends.
The Polish console often came to us, and when it happened, we immediately left home and went to the consulate.
And there we hid.
I don't remember if it was for a day or two days.
But all Polish Jews had been collected and had been dispatched to Poland.
Yes.
We did not-- we did not leave Munich.
We were not dispatched.
We were most fortunate.
But the Polish Jews who left had a very hard time because Poland would not accept them.
The train stood in no man's land for days.
There was no water, no sanitation, and then they returned to Germany.
Well, we were-- I have little recollection, but we were only there a day or two.
We returned home and from then onwards, our life continued as semi-normal as it could be.
Then came the Kristallnacht in 1938.
Well, that was reasonably, I would say, the end of organized Jewish life as such in Germany.
All the synagogues were burned except-- one in a few exceptions-- ours.
Ours was not touched for a simple reason.
There was-- it was built behind another block of flats, and had they dynamited our synagogue, the building in front would have gone up in flames as well.
So they left our synagogue untouched, and used it as a furniture store.
Of course it was ransacked.
Everything was plundered.
The scrolls-- the sacred Torahs-- everything was burned.
But the synagogue as such was untouched.
Many, many years later-- many years later-- on one of our journeys to Italy, I said to my wife, I'd like to go just to Munich, and we went.
We went to Munich to spend just one day there to go to synagogue and to go to the cemetery.
There was my seat where we sat, exactly as I'd left it.
I could have left my [INAUDIBLE] in there.
I doubt it.
And the-- and the synagogue was a synagogue again?
Yes.
Functioning again, large community, with a German police officer standing outside guarding it with a [INAUDIBLE] gun.
The Kristallnacht happened-- we were at home.
But it happened very quickly in ours.
Munich was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] awakened to fire engines, police cars racing about.
But there was nothing we could possibly do.
The large synagogues were dynamited and burned and plundered, and many, many Jews were rounded up and taken to concentration camps.
Not my father.
That was the end of '38.
On the fifth of January, 1939, only a few days before, my parents decided that I should better go.
There was no such thing as preparing.
There wasn't much to prepare because we didn't have much.
So a case was packed for me.
I was sent by train on Kindertransport, and I came to England.
We heard the noise, we heard the fire engines, but not much more.
We went to town and we could see everything was ablaze.
That was it.
That was the end.
Jewish shops were plundered and destroy.
The whole Jewish community was literally down.
Yes.
Definitely.
Great surprise.
Yes.
My parents made this decision and told me that they'd decided it was best for me to leave before we were going to America, because we still planned to go to America.
They considered my sister to be too young, and so--
She was five years younger, so she was 11, 12.
I came on my own with another group of children.
No.
I've no-- absolutely no idea of.
The-- my mother was very active in the community and that all happened within days.
My ca-- my mother was very active in the Jewish community, yes.
Very active.
My mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was active with the immigration of children to Israel-- to Palestine at the time-- and also to receive people coming back from concentration camp.
They had a community of women who stid-- stood at the platform to receive the men coming out, to give them food, to give them hope.
